principles.label: Standardized Consortium Approach
principles.summary: Comprehensive biological understanding requires a coordinated, standardized approach rather than isolated, bespoke experiments.
principles.details: When individual labs use their own uncoordinated techniques, they produce disconnected findings riddled with technical noise. A unified, shared approach is necessary to build foundational biological catalogs.
principles.representative_quote: ...only if we do it in a standardized way from sampling acquisition, to sample prep, to QC, to analytics would we ever have a chance of actually creating a meaningful catalog that is not riddled by noise. And our body didn’t hear that it actually belongs to different institutes.

principles.label: Cells as the Genotype-Phenotype Bridge
principles.summary: Cells are the critical intermediate between genetic variants and disease phenotypes.
principles.details: To functionally characterize genetic variants, understand how they cause disease, or perform effective gene therapy, we must know exactly which specific (and sometimes rare) cells those variants manifest in.
principles.representative_quote: Now, cells are also a key intermediate in between the genotype and the phenotypes that we care about so dearly... if we actually want to functionally characterize variants and act upon them we have to know which cells we are dealing with.

principles.label: Computation Before Collection
principles.summary: Computation must be integrated into experimental design before data collection, not just treated as a post-experiment analysis step.
principles.details: Statistical frameworks and power analyses are necessary to make decisions throughout every step of the process, ensuring the right number of cells and spatial patterns are captured.
principles.representative_quote: computation for the human cell Atlas is not just about what happens after the data has been collected it is as ever it is at every point and in particular before the data has been collected and the experiment has been designed

principles.label: AI for Noisy Biological Data
principles.summary: Massive, noisy biological data requires AI algorithms to extract meaningful insights.
principles.details: Modern lab technologies produce data at an unprecedented scale but with high levels of noise. Manual or traditional analysis is insufficient; AI is the necessary cost to make sense of this complexity.
principles.representative_quote: There’s no free lunch. You always have to pay. If we want to make sense of this noisy and messy data, we need AI algorithms

principles.label: Under-Sampled Inference
principles.summary: Exhaustive measurement of complex biological systems is impossible; researchers must leverage under-sampled data and cartographic scaling.
principles.details: Because of the combinatorial explosion of variables in biology, we cannot measure every cell or genetic interaction. By applying principles of cartography and low-dimensional inference, we can extract comprehensive understanding from highly under-sampled data.
principles.representative_quote: Overcoming this limitation is not simply a matter of reducing costs with existing approaches; for complex biological systems it will likely never be possible to comprehensively measure and perturb every combination of variables of interest.

principles.label: Maximize Cell Numbers Over Depth
principles.summary: In complex tissues, maximizing the number of cells analyzed is far more important than the sequencing depth per cell.
principles.details: To accurately cluster highly diverse populations and capture rare cell types, analyzing tens of thousands of cells shallowly is far more effective than analyzing a small number of cells deeply.
principles.representative_quote: large cell numbers are really critical when you have a complex tissue. the number of cells is probably the most the best thing you can do for yourself more so than the depth per cell

principles.label: Algorithm Dictates Insight
principles.summary: The choice of algorithm dictates the biological insight you uncover from a dataset.
principles.details: Applying different mathematical and AI approaches to the exact same dataset reveals fundamentally different biological phenomena, from static cell types to dynamic differentiation patterns.
principles.representative_quote: When algorithms cluster, we find cell types. When algorithms find continual trajectories or optimal transport maps, we see dynamical patterns, like cell differentiation, or tumorous cells.

principles.label: Navigating the Vast Therapeutic Space
principles.summary: The space of genetic and therapeutic possibilities is too vast to be explored purely through empirical testing.
principles.details: The number of possibilities exceeds what can ever be tested in a physical lab or clinical trial. Generative AI must be used to augment biological atlases by predicting missing information and generating therapeutic molecules de novo.
principles.representative_quote: ...there is an enormous space of genetic and therapeutic possibilities, which exceeds by many orders of magnitude what can ever be tested in a lab, clinical trial, or even an entire population.

principles.label: Scientific Ownership and Self-Starting
principles.summary: Scientists must own their destiny and learn to self-start from a lack of knowledge.
principles.details: Mentorship is not about teaching existing facts or managing tasks; it is about guiding mentees to navigate into the unknown. Scientists should not wait for others to define their curriculum or tell them how to solve a problem.
principles.representative_quote: you do not have to assume that somebody knows the answer of how to go after your problem but rather that you can just take the take your problem and run with it

principles.label: Interdisciplinary Rigor
principles.summary: Study interdisciplinary subjects at their maximum level of rigor.
principles.details: To effectively combine different disciplines, you must first understand them at their purest, most rigorous level (e.g., learning math from mathematicians) rather than settling for a watered-down version.
principles.representative_quote: you actually had to study it at its maximum level... at which point it was easier for you to bring the disciplines together

frameworks.label: Lab in a Loop
frameworks.summary: An iterative operational model for AI-driven drug discovery that integrates experiments, data, and algorithms.
frameworks.details: 1. Use experimental or clinical data to train computational models. 2. Use the trained models to predict the next set of experiments to run. 3. Iterate the process at scale to yield specific project predictions and globally improve the model.
frameworks.representative_quote: ...scientists use experimental or clinical data to train models, the models are used to predict the next set of experiments, and the process is iterated, at scale...

frameworks.label: Computational Modeling of Cellular Circuits
frameworks.summary: A method for deciphering how cellular networks and protein signals change in response to stimuli.
frameworks.details: 1. Gather dynamic gene expression data using single-cell RNA sequencing. 2. Subject cells to stimuli (hormones, pathogens). 3. Create algorithms to decipher the most likely sequence of molecular events. 4. Test predictions by silencing specific genes (e.g., via CRISPR) and observing the response.

frameworks.label: Cellular Atlas Dimensions
frameworks.summary: The required dimensions for building a comprehensive reference map of biological tissues.
frameworks.details: 1. Catalog all distinct cell types. 2. Distinguish between different states for the same cell type. 3. Position all cells in 3D spatial locations. 4. Identify salient features during dynamic transitions. 5. Trace the entire developmental lineage.
frameworks.representative_quote: Well, obviously the first thing that should be there is the listing of all of the different cell types... We also want to be able to distinguish between different states... position all of these cells in their three dimensional locations...

frameworks.label: Molecular-Morphological Validation Framework
frameworks.summary: A process for discovering and validating new cell types using unsupervised single-cell RNA-seq clustering.
frameworks.details: 1. Perform unsupervised clustering on single-cell RNA-seq data. 2. Identify differentially expressed marker genes for each cluster. 3. Use double-color FISH to confirm co-expression with known markers. 4. If no markers exist, use sparse morphological labeling combined with FISH to match the new molecular marker to expected morphology.
frameworks.representative_quote: we took each of the Clusters we predicted molecular markers... and now we're going to see whether the marker is actually expressed in the appropriate type

mental_models.label: The Fruit Smoothie vs. Fruit Salad
mental_models.summary: A metaphor contrasting traditional bulk genomics with single-cell genomics.
mental_models.details: Bulk analysis blends all cells together (a smoothie), losing individual cell identities and averaging out rare signals. Single-cell genomics analyzes each cell individually (a fruit salad), allowing researchers to distinguish distinct cell types and their unique genetic activation.
mental_models.representative_quote: What we can do today in single-cell genomics can be thought of as the era of the fruit salad with many different kinds of fruits and each of them analyzed separately and individually and very. very rapidly.

mental_models.label: Google Maps for the Human Body
mental_models.summary: A spatial and visual framework for biological data that allows researchers to navigate across scales.
mental_models.details: Like Google Maps, a comprehensive cell atlas allows researchers to zoom out to see relationships at the tissue and organ level, and zoom in to see extremely fine molecular details of individual cells.
mental_models.representative_quote: Just like in Google Maps, where you can zoom out and you see everything and you can zoom in and you can look at extremely fine detail, so would be the Human Cell Atlas.

mental_models.label: The Periodic Table of Cells
mental_models.summary: Viewing cells as the fundamental, categorizable base units of human biology.
mental_models.details: Just as the periodic table organizes chemical elements and allows for the prediction of undiscovered ones, a systematic classification of all cell types serves as a foundational reference tool that enables countless future biological discoveries.
mental_models.representative_quote: the base unit of our body is the cell and in that sense cells are akin to other elements that we know about in science in general and in biology you can think of them similar to the elements in a periodic table

mental_models.label: Cells as Computer Circuits
mental_models.summary: Viewing the complex molecular interactions within and between cells as 'circuits' or wiring diagrams.
mental_models.details: The cell's protein signaling networks process inputs (stimuli) and produce outputs (transcribing DNA), governing biological function in health and malfunction in disease. These can be systematically deciphered using computational algorithms and process algebra.
mental_models.representative_quote: It’s like a complex computer that is made of these many, many different parts that are interacting with each other and telling each other what to do.

mental_models.label: Liberating vs. Retrofitting
mental_models.summary: A lens for adopting new technologies by reimagining workflows rather than forcing new tools into old paradigms.
mental_models.details: Instead of forcing a new tool (like AI) into an existing, traditional workflow ('retrofitting'), one should reimagine the entire scientific process from the ground up to fully utilize the new capabilities ('liberating').
mental_models.representative_quote: Think about new things in new ways. Instead of trying to retrofit a new thing into the old way of doing things say, ‘How do we liberate it?'

mental_models.label: The Biological Atlas as a Foundation Model
mental_models.summary: Viewing a comprehensive dataset of human cells as the essential training data required to build computational models that 'understand' biology.
mental_models.details: A biological atlas is not merely a reference catalog; it provides the massive magnitude and scale of data needed to train foundational AI models of cellular behavior.
mental_models.representative_quote: ...having a map like this, an atlas as we call it, a data set of this magnitude and scale, would really allow us to build a model to understand cells. Today, we call them foundational models or foundation models.

mental_models.label: Pointillist Sampling & Low-Dimensional Inference
mental_models.summary: The understanding that a complete picture of a complex system can emerge from sparse but strategic sampling.
mental_models.details: Applying map-making principles to biology: just as a geographic map uses a scale rather than a 1:1 representation, a biological atlas can use statistical sampling to accurately represent a tissue without measuring every constituent cell.
mental_models.representative_quote: In the same sense that when we chart the map we actually don’t do it in a one-to-one scale, we do in it a one-to-one hundred, or one-to-one thousand, the level of resolution that is needed.

mental_models.label: Cell Type as a Probability Distribution
mental_models.summary: Conceptualizing a cell type mathematically as a region or probability distribution in a complex, multi-dimensional space.
mental_models.details: Instead of rigid, discrete categories, data-driven computational methods allow researchers to define cell types precisely as they go down to finer and finer biological distinctions.
mental_models.representative_quote: we can think of a cell type maybe as a region or a probability distribution in some complex multi-dimensional space

heuristics.label: Every cell is an experiment
heuristics.summary: Treat every individual cell as a distinct experiment to capture the full diversity of biological responses.
heuristics.representative_quote: Every cell is an experiment now.

heuristics.label: Go back in developmental time
heuristics.summary: When a cell's identity is confusing at its endpoint, trace it back through developmental time to reveal its true origin.
heuristics.representative_quote: when in doubt like that at the end point go back in time and try to use developmental biology to tell you where you came from

heuristics.label: Extract nuclei when cells fail
heuristics.summary: If you can't get intact cells out of a complex or dense tissue without destroying them, bypass the issue by extracting and sequencing the nuclei.

heuristics.label: Iterate when uncertain
heuristics.summary: When you don't know the answer ahead of time, take a guess, combine different methods, and iterate because no single method is perfect.
heuristics.representative_quote: like in many many problems in life when you actually don't know the answer ahead of time you're going to have to iterate you're going to have to take a guess and combine different methods because none of them is perfect

heuristics.label: Cells are multidimensional
heuristics.summary: Never look at one cellular process and forget the others; cells simultaneously have a type, a location, and undergo multiple transitions.

heuristics.label: Pilot before scaling
heuristics.summary: Start large-scale mapping projects with a pilot in a few complementary systems (e.g., blood, gut, liver) before scaling to the whole organism.

heuristics.label: Act without asking for advice
heuristics.summary: Sometimes it's best to pursue an idea without asking for advice, as common wisdom can steer you away from unconventional leaps.
heuristics.representative_quote: sometimes the best decisions are i made the best leaps i've made the things that obviously paid off the most were the things where maybe i was too ignorant to realize that i should ask someone for advice

heuristics.label: Liberate, don't retrofit
heuristics.summary: Instead of trying to retrofit a new technology into the old way of doing things, ask 'How do we liberate it?'
heuristics.representative_quote: Think about new things in new ways. Instead of trying to retrofit a new thing into the old way of doing things say, ‘How do we liberate it?'

heuristics.label: Embrace fields without textbooks
heuristics.summary: Seek out emerging fields where the rules aren't written yet; that is where your ideas can become 'the thing'.
heuristics.representative_quote: It’s an unbelievable privilege to be in that career stage when there aren’t textbooks and there isn’t a way things are done … Your ideas could be ‘the thing.’

signature_quotes.label: The Fruit Salad Era
signature_quotes.summary: Describing the paradigm shift of single-cell genomics.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: What we can do today in single-cell genomics can be thought of as the era of the fruit salad with many different kinds of fruits and each of them analyzed separately and individually and very. very rapidly.

signature_quotes.label: The Body Doesn't Belong to Institutes
signature_quotes.summary: Arguing for a unified, standardized consortium approach to biology.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: ...our body didn’t hear that it actually belongs to different institutes.

signature_quotes.label: Every Cell is an Experiment
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the impact of single-cell RNA sequencing on research.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Every cell is an experiment now.

signature_quotes.label: No Free Lunch with Noisy Data
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the trade-off of modern high-throughput technologies and the necessity of AI.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: There’s no free lunch. You always have to pay. If we want to make sense of this noisy and messy data, we need AI algorithms.

signature_quotes.label: Google Maps for the Human Body
signature_quotes.summary: Describing the utility and scale of the Human Cell Atlas.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Just like in Google Maps, where you can zoom out and you see everything and you can zoom in and you can look at extremely fine detail, so would be the Human Cell Atlas.

signature_quotes.label: Conquering Fear
signature_quotes.summary: Reflecting on bravery in scientific pursuits.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: being brave is not about being fearless it is actually about conquering your fear and doing things despite being afraid

signature_quotes.label: Liberating Technology
signature_quotes.summary: Encouraging students to think creatively about how to apply AI in biology.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Think about new things in new ways. Instead of trying to retrofit a new thing into the old way of doing things say, ‘How do we liberate it?'

signature_quotes.label: Cells as the Periodic Table
signature_quotes.summary: Introducing the foundational concept behind the Human Cell Atlas.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: the base unit of our body is the cell and in that sense cells are akin to other elements that we know about in science in general and in biology you can think of them similar to the elements in a periodic table

signature_quotes.label: Atlases as Foundation Models
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the computational foresight behind building the Human Cell Atlas.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: ...having a map like this, an atlas as we call it, a data set of this magnitude and scale, would really allow us to build a model to understand cells. Today, we call them foundational models or foundation models.

signature_quotes.label: Cell Numbers Over Depth
signature_quotes.summary: Advising on experimental design for single-cell RNA sequencing.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: large cell numbers are really critical when you have a complex tissue. the number of cells is probably the most the best thing you can do for yourself more so than the depth per cell

anti_patterns.label: Fragmented Biological Mapping
anti_patterns.summary: Building foundational biological datasets through fragmented, unstandardized efforts across different labs.
anti_patterns.details: It introduces massive technical noise and batch effects, making it impossible to distinguish true biological variation from differences in sample prep or analytics.

anti_patterns.label: Relying Exclusively on Bulk Sequencing
anti_patterns.summary: Relying exclusively on bulk RNA sequencing to understand cellular function and diversity.
anti_patterns.details: It pools RNA from millions of cells together, blending the data so that it is impossible to distinguish the specific gene expression of individual, potentially rare, cell types.

anti_patterns.label: Over-Sequencing Single Cells
anti_patterns.summary: Insisting on deep sequencing for every cell in a complex tissue.
anti_patterns.details: Due to the conversion rate limits of current protocols, sequencing too deeply mostly yields duplicate reads, wasting money for diminishing returns and limiting the total number of cells you can afford to process.

anti_patterns.label: Retrofitting New Technologies
anti_patterns.summary: Retrofitting new technologies into old ways of doing things.
anti_patterns.details: It limits the potential of the new technology. By just plugging a new tool into an existing experimental setup, scientists miss the opportunity to completely reimagine and optimize the scientific process.

anti_patterns.label: Computation as an Afterthought
anti_patterns.summary: Treating computation solely as a post-experiment data analysis step.
anti_patterns.details: It misses the opportunity to use statistical frameworks for power analysis and experimental design, which are crucial for determining how many cells to profile or how to detect spatial patterns.

anti_patterns.label: Restricting Search to Prior Knowledge
anti_patterns.summary: Restricting the search space of genetic and therapeutic possibilities based solely on prior knowledge.
anti_patterns.details: While practical historically, it severely limits the ability to make new discoveries because the true possibility space is much larger than what is already known.

anti_patterns.label: Always Asking for Advice
anti_patterns.summary: Always asking for advice before pursuing a new idea or direction.
anti_patterns.details: Asking for advice often yields 'common wisdom' that can steer you away from unconventional ideas and temper your natural enthusiasm and curiosity.
